snippets.title: CSV with dtypes, dates, and column pruning
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv(
    "transactions.csv",
    usecols=["txn_id", "user_id", "amount", "merchant_id", "fraud_label", "ts"],
    dtype={
        "txn_id": "string",
        "user_id": "category",
        "merchant_id": "category",
        "amount": "float32",
        "fraud_label": "int8",
    },
    parse_dates=["ts"],
    na_values=["", "NA", "null"],
)
print(df.dtypes)
print("MB:", df.memory_usage(deep=True).sum() / 1e6)
snippets.explanation: Explicit dtypes prevent IDs from becoming floats and shrink numeric columns. `usecols` skips unused fields entirely.

snippets.title: Parquet: selective column read
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_parquet(
    "warehouse/clicks.parquet",
    columns=["session_id", "item_id", "clicked", "event_time"],
    engine="pyarrow",
)
print(df.head())
snippets.explanation: Parquet reads only requested columns from disk—efficient for wide tables where models use a small feature subset.

snippets.title: JSON Lines to flat table
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.read_json("api_events.jsonl", lines=True)
flat = pd.json_normalize(raw.to_dict(orient="records"))
flat = flat.astype({"event_name": "category", "user_id": "string"})
print(flat.columns.tolist()[:6])
snippets.explanation: Nested JSON becomes columns via normalization; cast high-cardinality strings to category where appropriate.

snippets.title: Memory audit after load
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("big.csv")  # naive load for comparison
usage = df.memory_usage(deep=True)
print(usage.sort_values(ascending=False).head())
print(f"Total GB: {usage.sum() / 1e9:.2f}")
snippets.explanation: Identify object columns and float64 fields to target for dtype fixes on the next load or via downcasting.

snippets.title: Load then isolate train rows (awareness pattern)
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_parquet("labeled.parquet").sort_values("event_time")
n = len(df)
train_end = int(0.7 * n)

train_df = df.iloc[:train_end].copy()
holdout_df = df.iloc[train_end:].copy()

# Fit any vocabulary / imputation on train_df only before touching holdout_df
mean_amount = train_df["amount"].mean()
train_df["amount"] = train_df["amount"].fillna(mean_amount)
snippets.explanation: Full load is OK when you immediately partition and compute statistics on train only. Split mechanics live in the evaluation topic.
